segments.text: Away you go, we're happy to see you from one of those sorts of people.
Porina tātu.
Porina.
Tena kata e a kotea Warbrick toku ingoa,
geiria hono te alawa iwi a tearoa waka,
ko Ngāti Rangitahi, Te Raurangi Ngāti Wahi o me tuwharetoa o ku iwi,
and I'm the DCE Strategic Engagement Māori Partnerships
and Data Capability at Stats Indeed.
Porina.
Morina tātu.
Ko Anna McDow toku ingoa, ko Te Atiawa, Ngāti Kahanunu,
Ngāti Mania Pouto, Ngāi Tuhoe o koe iwi,
I'm the General Manager Strategic Engagement Māori Partnerships
and Outcomes at Stats Indeed.
Excellent.
We're also joined on the screen by Rachel Wilcox,
and Rachel is our Principal Advisor,
Data Operations and Infrastructure,
unfortunately not able to be here in person today,
so thank you for allowing her to be online.
We're good.
Excellent.
Yeah, you just, if you'd like to just present,
just give us a name for it,
and we've had the opportunity to read through
the brief document that we've provided for the meeting,
and, yeah, go from there, so thank you.
Good bye.
I'll walk you there, and thank you for the invitation
to brief you on the Integrated Data Infrastructure
or what we commonly call the IDI.
We realised that the IDI is pretty complex,
so we thought we'd just spend a short amount of time this morning
highlighting some of the key points
that we've provided in the information that you've received.
We thought that would be a really useful sort of framework
for opening up for conversation.
We've allowed, yeah, we'll be very brief,
to allow and ensure that there's plenty of time for questions
in the end, but if you'd prefer a more interactive session,
please feel free to jump in and ask us questions
as we're just walking through the intro.
Before we get into the detail around the IDI,
we thought it might be really helpful to share a bit of context
about the environment we're working in at Stats Endent.
So I think, you know, it's reasonably well understood
and recognised that...
that current government data systems don't deliver as well for Iwi y Māori,
not as well as we'd like.
This impacts the quality of data at national and regional levels
and means that Māori are not always accurately and meaningfully represented in data.
That can of course impact the quality of decision making for government
as well as for Iwi themselves.
Māori haf consistently told government that they don't have all the data that they need to achieve their aspirations.
In many cases this affects their ability to design, deliver and evaluate services that work for their communities.
Sometimes the data does exist within government systems but it's not readily accessible or usable in ways that can support Māori to understand how they can deliver or identify their priorities.
In terms of the levers utilised by STATS NZ, we have a strategic, enterprise-wide focus on delivering better data outcomes for and with Māori iwi and hapū.
This sees a strong emphasis on our TREA settlement and a called work with iwi and other government agencies,

the mana Ōtiti relationship that we have with the data iwi leaders group and of course working across STATS to ensure our staff are better equipped to recognise and act on data opportunities that will benefit iwi Māori.
We also have legislative levers that can be used to drive change across the government data system.
The Data and Statistics Act 2022 includes provisions that are designed to address current data system failings
and to ensure more representative government data system into the future.
Section 14 of the Act Specific way requires that the government status-tush in, who is also currently out the air of STATS NZ, recognise the interests of Māori to build STATS capability.
to engage and understand Māori interests and data,
but also to foster the capability of Māori
to collect data, produce statistics,
and use data for research and engage with stats NZ.
That's probably enough for me.
You're comfortable if I hand over
to get more into the IDO?
Absolutely.
Thanks, Tata.
So I will highlight probably just a few elements of the IDO.
As Tia said, it's bit complex.
So we'll work our way through it
and then also talk briefly but more specifically
about the work that we currently do
with Iwi and Māori as well in that space.
So as you have seen, we at stats NZ
maintain two integrated databases.
One about people and households,
which is the IDI, and our focus today.
And the other, the Longitudinal Business Database,
or the LVD, which is about businesses.
Those two databases do talk to each other
through the Inland Revenue Information.
So we can link employer and employee information.
And so people do do research
that covers both of those databases.
The IDI contains a range of data about events, services,
so education, income, benefits, migration, justice, health.
There's data in there from across all of those areas.
And the data itself comes from government agencies,
from stats NZ, so the census,
and pretty much all of our social survey information is in there.
There was also some non-government organisation data in the IDI.
In terms of history, we've been integrating data at stats NZ
since 1997.
And about that time, there was a Cabinet directive
that actually if you're going to integrate
more than one data set together,
that stats NZ was a good place to do that.
And a large amount of the reasoning behind that
is that the legislation that we do have
that enables that to be done safely.
At the time, we were very much about...

te iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi iwi
wē starta te sīsa mō signifikata expansion.
It was the time of better public service measures
and social investment emerging.
And so we looked really hard at how we needed to grow it,
how we did that safely and how we get more analytical capability
being able to be utilised in the IDI.
That was also when we moved to a broader space
than a labour market focus.
And so for the first time you could see more children
and old people in the IDI as well.
And that's really important in terms of
what you would have seen in the briefing.
There's a central linking spine in the IDI.
So that aims to create an ever-resident population.
So it's bigger than the population of New Zealand
because it's not a resident right now situation.
And that works
because there are three data sets that we link together
to create the spine.
And so we have birth registrations
from the Department of Internal Affairs.
We have visa or migration information from MB.
And we also have tax data from in-led revenue.
We link those three data sets together
to create our spine
and then all other event-type data sets
or information is linked to the spine.
There are two ways in which we do the linking in the IDI
and without getting into too much of the technical detail
the two are one.
we can directly or deterministic link data, so that's where there is a unique identifier
and two sets of data that we can link together directly. So, for example, your NHI number from
the health system or your inland revenue number from the tax system, if those identifiers exist
on two datasets, we will directly link. The other way in which we link, which is
probabilistic, which is where we use name, sex, date of birth, and increasingly address
information to link datasets together. There's more opportunity for a bit of noise or error
in that linking, so we work really hard to keep on top of that and make sure that we're
really clear about the amount of error in that sort of linking. Each time we refresh the IDI,
we collect data from almost, I think, now 30 data supplies. This is all done via
secure file transfer and the data is on the whole fully resupplied each time we update the IDI.
There's a lot of quality checking and work that we do to ensure that we're able to do
this the best we can. We remove all of the identifiable information before we make it
available for research use and I'll get under that a little bit in a second.
We update the IDI itself three times each year. We do that in March, June and October.
There are some data loads that we do more frequently and that is because the
unique identifier is on that data and that unique identifier is already in the IDI
and that includes things like some of the tax information comes through more regularly than
the quarterly update. There are two guiding
pieces of legislation that underpin our ability to be able to safely manage and provide access
to the IDI and that is the Data and Statistics Act 2022 and the Privacy Act 2020. The IDI is often
described as being privacy by design in terms of how we put it together and so that means we
build and provide access to the IDI primarily ensuring that individuals information is remained
confidential. That's great. It also at times causes us some difficulty for example if someone
wants to understand exactly what data of theirs as an individual is in the IDI we'd have to unpick
quite a significant amount of work to do that so there are always balances to be had with a
database that's as broad and detailed as the IDI so we work really hard to maintain the safety
parameters around it. We have absolutely suggesting that an individual has a right to know what's
information is recorded against you. How is an individual on that out? What's the process?
It's pretty difficult because of the way we pull it together it is something that we would like to
be able to do much more easily. It actually at this stage isn't generally that easy and would probably
be it's more efficient to actually be able to understand what the source data is that you might
have in the IDI and work it through that way. So the privacy mechanisms that we put in place
ensure that privacy at that group level and at the individuals when data is coming out
it does mean that because of the mechanisms we've got in place we can't easily I couldn't easily if I
requested it I couldn't easily find out I've got that record that record and that record from these
service provisions that are in the IDI. In your histories.
whether there have been any problems with privacy?
Like any privacy incidents?
Any…
No, we haven't had any yeah yeah.
We and actually that segways
really nicely into the frameworks we use
to protect privacy and the safety of the data.
So we have two
that we use, one has been around
for longer than the i di and that's the 5 safe framework
and we outlined that in the brief.
The five SACES is designed to work like a bit of a team, so it's about the safe people, safe projects, safe settings, safe data and safe outputs.
And so we look at the settings across the five and so for example, the data we provide access to for researchers is de-identified, so there is no identifying information remained on.

There's still a lot of information in there, and so by making sure that let's, for example, they're in a setting, a physical setting and technology settings that are controlled and we've also got relationships with each of the researchers.
So there's a trust environment there, then we're using the group of the five SACES collectively to manage our responsibilities.
So an issue, just chip in. Anybody chip in?
Yeah, please do. Sorry.
Thank you, Mr Chair.
And just another question about the Privacy Act requirements.
So the other requirement is the ability to correct data that's held as well.
Is that a function that you're looking at?
And then the second part was there's currently a bill before the House on amendments to privacy laws in terms of indirect collection of data from indirect sources.
Is that something that you're kind of pre-empting in terms of the requirements there?
The first bit I think I can probably tackle, and I think the second bit we might have to ask general counsel for some people or a policy manager.
So in terms of correct...
information, we would go back to the source and ask for the information to be corrected
at the source so that the agency who supplies the data, because we're getting those new
supplies through, we can correct that that way.
Thank you, Sarah. This is Sarah, general councillor.
Kia ora, kata. I'm Sarah Duen Toko, i'm general manager, risk and assurance and general
councillor at Stats, and quite a quick answer for you is that our understanding at the moment
is that new requirement will have an exemption for an legislation, because it would be quite
difficult, but we are still thinking about how that's going to work with us, with our
partners, and also hoping there's some guidance from the Privacy Commission as well about
your expectations around that. So we'll see you with then.
Thank you.
Sarah?
Happy for me to clarify for a little bit. I won't take too much more time. So we've got
the five safes. Our other framework we use is Ngati Kungapai Hedi, and that's really
about ensuring that we bring the people and their stories that sit behind the data
to the forefront of our processes and the researchers who use the data. So it encourages safe
and ethical use of data by considering the impact on people the data is about and
encouraging relationships and collaboration and certainly sharing of
results with the population of interest that research projects are about. The two
frameworks we use are really complementary, and they really help our researchers to
understand their responsibilities and how they can work with communities
across New Zealand to get the most value out of the data. In terms of the
access to the data in a more concrete way, we have data lab facilities where we
do that. We have 42 of them across the country at the moment, and they
range. We've got three in our own Stats NZ offices. Many government
departments will have the data.
academic institutions, there are some research, private sector research firms, and one EWI Data Lab
that was set up in 2023 that our partners Te Kahuera Runga of the Data EWI Leaders Group
managed in Rotorua. We support currently 12,000 researchers across some 370 projects.
That's a pretty huge increase since the approximately 15 projects in the 2011
version of the IDI. Over that time, there hasn't been significant changes in the technology
or the methods we've used for the IDI, but there's been a very firm focus on ensuring
that our processes and protocols have evolved over that time, that we've worked with the
researchers to ensure that we've been able to stay relevant and current.
With that amount of use, it's not surprising that the IDI is pretty embedded across government
and beyond now in terms of a critical data tool. It's used, many agencies now rely on the IDI to
inform service planning and delivery and program evaluation. For example, the IDI hosts simulations
and models to research policy change and for monitoring outcomes in treasury.
There's a tax welfare model, an MSD, we have the social outcomes model, and with Oranga Tamariki,
we have the children's well-being model. The IDI is a critical success tool for a social
investment approach, and we're already working with the social investment agency on this.
The IDI holds individual level data that can enable cohort and time series analysis
of factors that impact people's lives and needs. Importantly,
at enables research of factors across different sectors and different outcomes for people.
Back to the five safes in Ngatekanga Paihiri, our frameworks, you cannot output data
that identifies individuals and it cannot be used for case management
and that links back to the data and stats.
By case management you cannot use the IDI to intervene in somebody's life,
whether that be for a regulatory purpose or because you want to provide
some sort of beneficial service.
There is emerging use of the IDI by Iwi, for example,
to establish monitoring and reporting against strategies
and to make decisions about service provision investment.
A recent count of projects, the number of Iwi-affiliated
active research projects at 16, the IDI is also a really important tool
for stats and we're increasingly using it for statistical production.
As long as you're happy, time-wise, I can talk a little bit more specifically
in brief about the work we're doing with Iwi and Ngati with the IDI.
I think what I'd like is this is excellent for just getting an understanding
of what God used the word tool, what this tool is all about,
but also to explore the future opportunities of what this means for.
We are the Māori Affairs Committee, it's been a decent amount of time
in that space but I'll let you run this to achieve that in means,
if you can please.
What we wanted to highlight was the ways in which Iwi and Ngati
using it and where there is certainly room for growth.
So the amount of data collected and in different ways
and for different purposes
betu ata rungitaach.
Aroi mehanai ita ateipi te kaitia
Ido i ma ni, te kaitia
rungita�u i kiitimiai o aatimiai
a kaitia k Marianne Taradang
Iroi te kaitia kaitia.

i kanada'i pāpoha kai
kaitia kaitia pwahani

i kaitia kaitia

i kaitia pwahani
i kaitia kaitia
i kaitia kaitia
i kaitia kaitia
i kaitia kaitia
i kaitia kaitia

So, working directly with EE, on agreed data plans that talk to their aspirations and the relevant data needs for them is common for us to help define what data is needed, identify where that data exists, not always is it only in the IDI and provide assistance with getting access to it and working with the EE data analysts.

The IDI data is often required to address cross-cutting topics of interest for EE, for example, and EE might want to understand the outcomes for Komatua in Rohe who have been hospitalised due to a heart attack.

This would require data to find people of a certain age, within a geographic area of interest, probably hospitalisation, even some mortality data, as well as potentially data about maybe subsequent benefit receipt to name a few.
This is the only place that that information is pulled together.
It's important, again, that we work with EE to grow their capability and how they can navigate this data space so that they're able to do that work without us as time goes by.
Not everyone can get into the data lab and certainly not straight away, so we also provide customised data as services for EE and wider.
These respond directly to EE data needs and the IDI is often called upon and the inclusion of census data in the IDI means that they're...
maitha ki i garao daru raitu i ariau, moun i maitha mari te tithurai i i bytau i i wani.
Ia maithi pinaupatu i i pa.
Marama pa, moun i i bytau i i bytau.
Daru mkokora pa raitu,
paito i i bytau i i bytau i bytau i bytau i bytau pinausa i bytau i bytau i bytau i bytau i bytau i bytau i bytau i bytau i bytau.
te akses a data now?
Yeah, so they're not everywhere.
I think that's the important thing tonight.
There's clearly many of them across government.
As I mentioned, there's one EE-based data lab in Ngorotaroa.
So they're not everywhere.
So sometimes people have to get to one.
So it's physical?
Yeah, it's a physical space.
The system is in Ireland, isn't it?
It's not connected to the internet.
That's correct, yeah.
So it's a physical space.
So there's the physical setting.
There's also controls around the technology settings.
So it's not connected to the internet.
You know, you can't access everything you'd like
in conjunction with the IDI.
When you're working in there, you're working on the IDI.
There are processes to check for confidentiality.
So making sure we're maintaining privacy
before any information is released
out of the data lab as well.
So yeah, there's the physical and technological settings
as well as the relationships
that we build with the researchers.
So they all complete training.
They all sign confidentiality agreements
to show they understand their responsibilities.
There's a number of factors that we work through
to ensure the safety.
And as in most things, it's a balance.
So it's how do you get more labs out there,
more ability for more people to use it
and make sure that the processes
and safety settings we have are going to be scaled
and work as we expand.
These are specific people employed or contracted via stats, et cetera.
So don't have to be contracted
or employed by stats, in fact.
the majority are not but part of our five safe frameworks is that researchers who
they do need to be big bona fide researchers they need to have some experience
they we we do reference checks when they first come in to use the data lab or the IDI yeah
and for example it's not uncommon to have PhD students and so what happens usually is
one of their supervisors is an experienced IDI user and so you know you we help make those
like those connections in the relationships they are really important as well.
All right thank you Mr Chair.
So yes we have services to provide customized data and I think also we provide there are some
there are some specific pieces of work to build EE and Māori data capability more
generally that are on the rise so through the mana Ōtiti relationship with Tākāhūrā
don't know the data iwi leaders group that we mentioned there is a comprehensive capability
building program for Te Mana Whakatepu this is where the iwi collections in the 2023 census
would lead and facilitated and also where our data apprenticeships and scholarships are
provided there is a specific piece of work in Te Mana Whakatepu on IDI capability
and last year Te Kāhūrā Dunga had I think 10 iwi data analysts who completed IDI training directly
learning about the processes how to handle the data and also each of them undertook a
research project that was agreed with their iwi this initiative I think was well oversubscribed
so the interest is really really high and and honestly the feedback really positive
from those who are taking part and from their iwi so this is something that we're proud
to partner with and contribute to
In terms of the sort of thing that Iwi are doing currently in the IDI, there's a variety of work.
So, for example, it has been used for recovery efforts after Cyclone Gabriel where access to an analysis of the IDI enabled Iwi leaders to make solid cases about housing needs post the Cyclone event.
The need to understand outcomes for the people that cut across government sectors and therefore dominant data sets is really important.
And another example would be Iwi wanting to understand how the Rangatahi are achieving in the education sector.
Without the IDI, Iwi would need to go to individual agencies to understand what data there is,
to then work out how they access it and not have a central point to be able to access it.
So, even being able to perhaps data that talks to their own strategies, the IDI provides an excellent space to be able to do that.
So, just before you move off that, you mentioned about housing and the situation of a natural event such as the Cyclone.
Yeah.
That would seem to me quite a sort of a time critical type of.
Absolutely.
And so, with your updating of your database every three times a year, how do you get the timeliness out of that?
Yeah.
So, we had to work really hard on that.
And so, it's safe to say that not all the data would have been as up to date, as would have been ideal.
So, it's about making the best out of it.
We certainly do in situations like that work with agencies to see what we can do.
So, let me ask the question a different way.
You have the ability that clearly this kind of information is going to be would be very handy in the next couple of weeks.
And you can instigate a process where this department please provide us your database on that.
In other words, can you expedite the results, which would be so helpful?
for the likes of e-read leaders about getting this information.
Not across the board. It depends on the data you need
and the outcome or the research question that you're looking at.
So no, we wouldn't be able to across the board expedite data to that extent.
Yep. So there is an element of, I won't want a bit of words,
sluggishness with regards to what you can actually achieve.
So when you've got time critical information to be able to act tomorrow,
it's not necessarily the best tool for that sometimes.
That's a very fair statement.
Okay, absolutely.
I think just to finish, we know that we need to balance the potential value alongside safety
and that's for all our youth and it is also relevant for iwi and Māori.
The challenges in the Government data systems tend to be noticeable really quickly in the
IDI because of the breadth and the depth of data in there
and because of the vast amount of research questions that are potentially possible.
So in many ways the IDI often acts like a barometer for some of the quality issues
with the data system more generally.
So it means we need to continue to work closely with Māne
to ensure we're always looking for ways to evolve our processes.
Our safety parameters and our governance structures
as we've talked about, we've already got frameworks in place.
They're really, really important.
They're not the only thing and they're not a replacement for the relationships
and the joint work and also the understanding of the context
that our communities are working in.
So that's something that we do have a keen eye on as well.
Excellent.
Thank you, Mr Chair.
I'll just tell a question about data security
and do you categorise types of data
and sort of enable security and protections on the basis?
of things like sensitivity. So you spoke earlier about health data and appeals to me like that might
be a category where additional safety measures are placed in terms of storage and potentially also
disclosure. And so Rachel online may want to chip in because she might have some more specific
information than me on this but there are a couple of things we do. So before any data set comes
into the IDI part of our privacy and ethics processes is also working out the confidentiality
requirements so how that data can be released from the lab and for some the settings are higher than
others because of the sensitivity of the data. We don't necessarily have different security settings
in how we store the data we're keeping that all as secure as we can. We do make sure that
the researchers have access to the data they need for their research questions rather than all of it.
So and you know and particularly keen on that when it is sensitive information.
Just interested around a similar corridor but slightly tangential in terms of the work you do
on tikanga pai here and interested in data sovereignty and what work might be underway
or being done to kind of enact that in a practical sense. So my first question I guess is like
what are the outputs of that? How do you monitor and report on it? Is there a metric for it
or is it just a nice ideological thing that is like we have these tikanga and that's great.
Are there some specific things you could describe for us and is there any work being done with
in terms of data sovereignty and then finally I'm just interested to know where the data is stored.
Is it in Aotearoa specifically or does any ever go offshore?
kapa? Maikata handle the last one first, yes, all the RDO data are stored on shore kapa,
yep. Do you want to?
Maikata ka wala mana auditie, if you didn't talk about the relationship, yep, so kapa.
So, as part of the mana auditie relationship that we have with data ILG and through their
charitable trust to kāhui iraranga, the data ILG released the Māori Data Governance
Model in May 2023. If you're familiar with the model, you'll know that it outlines
standards that enable Māori to participate meaningfully in data decision-making
and management. Stats NZ currently is providing ongoing support to government
agencies to raise awareness of the model and understand what opportunities there
might be in the separate parts of the data system across government. And we also
are continuing work with the data ILG to build a, sorry, I need my glasses on, I can't read my notes.
Excuse me, that's zero, but I was doing well. So, yeah, the strong focus on data
ethics in the model also really closely aligns with the needs of the data
system to consider assumes of ethics more generally, particularly in terms of data
use and innovation, which is a space that Stats NZ is at in the moment, for
example, in using integrated data for the purposes of social investment. I think,
you know, in terms of the Māori Data Governance Model at the moment, our role
really is to support and ready the government data system to support that
key work that I mentioned earlier. But we are also developing with the data ILG
and Māori Data Classification Framework. And what that framework will do is it
will help us to better understand the level and types of Māori data and
interest that might exist within a data set because that's not currently very evident to the moment
when you talk about Māori data if people want to know well what is Māori data what you're talking about
so that's a key focus at the work at the moment. The model itself has not been implemented like
agreed by government and implemented sort of as a wholesale model but it is a model that we
recognise provides us with significant information and detail about how to support
hold of system transformation for ensuring a data system that has more of a totality focused
and or Māori data aspirations embedded in it. Do you want to talk then maybe about
some of the work in more detail or is that helpful? I think nā te kāngapai heidi so I don't think
to the best of my knowledge there's not any specific monitoring or reporting on that however
every single research application for the IDI undergoes there's an element of that that is
directly relevant to nā te kāngapai heidi and the way the application process works is that the team
facilitating that work so hard with the researchers to make sure that they understand
their responsibilities and that they are living up to the requirements that we don't
like reject if you look for one of a better word many applications
but there is a variable amount of work that needs to be to be done depending on the expertise
the experience and the likes of researchers. I just have a supplementary question.
Business Chair, thank you that answered my questions comprehensively. I am interested
speaking about the access and how it's not a particularly accessible system currently
and in relation to security measures that are taken appropriately so.
to limit who has access to it in order to protect that data.
So things like having bona fide researchers, I was a PhD student
and understand the process is very rigorous to access things.
But in a Māori setting, obviously sometimes there are community groups
like Papakaina organisations or collectives of our people
who need to access that data but may not be considered bona fide
or legit in a Western framework for research.
And you talked about wanting to work actively to limit impacts of complexity.
So are there aspects of or are there people in this process
that are kind of considering that or doing stuff already to enable
those people to access through those pathways they may not otherwise
have been able to go through?
Yeah, that's a great question.
And I think the first most succinct comment is
we certainly haven't nailed it.
Yeah, it's still work in progress.
And in reality, not everyone wants to be in a data lab
to be able to use it.
Even in the government policy analysts want to be able to get
some value from it without having to have that capability too.
So we still need to work with our colleagues
across the public service on how we can get more value
out of the type of data, integrated data that's in the IDI outside,
so safely outside of the data lab environment.
And so I think that's one thing that will be really useful
